g4_revenue_trend.description: Revenue moved from $126.0B in 2017 to $130.9B in 2018 to $131.9B in 2019. The wireless base looked mature, but there was no obvious structural collapse at the decision point.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: Wireless and broadband economics moved around with handset mix and accounting presentation, but there was no clean two-year structural margin deterioration visible in the pre-2021 public filings.

g7_moat.description: Verizon had real assets in spectrum, towers, brand, and postpaid customer relationships, but the overall business still sat inside a capital-intensive oligopoly where required reinvestment and competition make the moat less pristine than a Buffett tollbooth.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — heavier strategic drift and weaker capital discipline than Verizon.
PASS — far more obvious structural decline and franchise erosion.

buffett_action: Berkshire reported 146.7M Verizon shares worth about $8.62B at 2020-12-31, then increased the stake to 158.8M shares by 2021-03-31.
actual_result: Berkshire still held 158.8M shares at 2021-12-31, but the position no longer appeared by 2022-12-31. This was large, but not permanent.
key_insight: A statistically cheap-looking telecom can still be a pass when the moat is not clean enough and the starting return only barely misses the hurdle.
